title: 2025 12 03 - HE (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Health Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Health [Part 1]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Health
description: Wednesday 3 December 2025 | Health Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Health Hearing of evidence: Hon Casey Costello, Minister for Seniors and Associate Minister of Health Minister for Mental Health Hon Matt Doocey
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Health. Hon Casey Costello, Minister for Seniors and Associate Minister of Health. Minister for Mental Health

chunks.text: Honorable Minister, director General, Dr. Bramley and everybody else, thank you for making yourself available this morning. We have you in here for 30 minutes. I will allow you to make some opening remarks, and then we will open it up to your members for questions. Got it. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, yeah, I will just make a couple of brief comments.

Um, as Associate Minister of Health, my focus this year has been on delivering practical improvements across my areas of responsibility, which include aged care, the development of a modern regulatory framework for medicines and medical devices, um, maternal and women's health, emergency road and air services, and our smoke-free goal.

Um, but what I want to talk about initially this morning is just the aged care, um, program of work. And I'd like to first of all thank the committee for the work you did in this space and the production of the report. Um, obviously there'll be a formal response, um, to that report, but in, in summary, you have, um, covered some key areas and the recommendations are in line with many of the things that we've been talking about, that I've heard about, and, um, compliments the direction of travel.

We definitely need more flexibility in the system so that it provides the help people need. We need to ensure that the community and in-home care services are set up the right way so that people can stay well at home as long as they can and want to. And, um, that's the right option. We need more aged care beds. Absolutely.

We need more respite and increased capacity to provide specialist dementia, psychogeriatric, and palliative care, and for the sector to be funded to provide these services and invest in building more. And we need better pathways out of hospital for older people. And from what I've seen, that starts with getting the admission process right and having upfront specialist advice considering the individual patient's needs from the outset.

In short, consistent with your report, our system for aged care needs to be one where people can access services they need more easily and transition between these services in a way that improves health, um, outcomes and quality of life. These are the issues for the Aged Care Ministerial Advisory Group. We'll be working through next year with this committee's report, part of their consideration.

So thank you, Mr. Chair, and have each take questions. Thank you, minister Dr. Vel. Thank you, minister. Um, and as you noted, the committee's taken a lot of time to go into the issues around aged care. You've mentioned the, um, issue of discharge of, um, elderly people from hospital into aged care.

Can you please, is help us prioritize the top three high impact actions that can be taken, uh, that, that you are taking to, uh, uh, speed discharge out of hospital for people to, to aged care? I, I think there, there is, um, in terms of actions, I think there is already actions in place and we've got some very good programs of work that exist as, as you'll be aware, um, hospital has a an excellent program around the admission for through ed.

Um, the Wellington Hospital has an excellent program around the surgical supporting the surgical unit. So those are, those are the, um, priorities I think is the geriatrician, um, in integration with that process, I think is really important. The, the, the, you know, actions in terms of differentiating from BAU around what, what is the, um, the Ministry and Health New Zealand's program work around workforce, et cetera.

Those are all the challenges that, that they will be responding to. But what we are focused on is, is looking at those, um, models of excellence that we know exist and how we can expand those opportunities. Um, Auckland Hospital also has an excellent program involving the geriatricians social workers. Um, and what we've seen is it does produce excellent results in terms of lowering readmission as well as shortening hospital stays.

And that's so, so therefore we know those models exist and therefore it's expanding on that. Um, having identified them, do you currently have a program to expand them that is funded across the country, or is that under development at the moment That that is under development? So this is, um, you know, the, the individual hospitals working through what is achievable within their budgets and their processes.

But, um, again, we, we will support those initiatives as part of this, um, aged care review. Do, Uh, this is a big issue. The committee's heard from many submitters on it and, and when we wrote our report, do you or your officials who are with you today have a sense of the resource required to make progress against this, um, this issue?

We haven't, we haven't got any numbers that I'm aware of. I'm not sure if they, from an official perspective, this isn't, um, a, a a separate piece of work that's securing that this is a priority across all the sectors because aged care is a, um, a big consumer of services. Um, but we have seen significant funding uplift into this, um, program at work, particularly in the age care sector Also as resource. Could you, could you explain the funding please?

So this is for the Aged Care, um, funding uplifts that To the understood sector. Alright. And, uh, you mentioned in your opening re remarks a ministerial advisory group. What are you hoping to get out of that group? So the group, the objective is to have it stood up by January, um, to report back no later than the middle of the year.

We, we are expecting it to report back in May. We've given a very tight timeline, um, and this is the opportunity to actually look at, you know, a lot of the recommendations that have come from your report. But what we know is that, you know, we have a high demand of aged care beds, but the system isn't sustainable as it sits at the moment.

We know that the contracting model isn't fit for purpose any further. It's been around for a long time. So it's a developing system, um, that supports, um, in-home community care, um, transition through from hospital, um, aged care because it's about balancing, you know, decreasing the demand for aged care beds, as well as increasing the supply of those beds has kind of got to work complementary to each other.

So the program of work is, is that system system design that we're trying to achieve in this space. Mr. One more? Yeah, thank you. And, um, uh, that sounds very, very, uh, technical and I'm sure you'll identify good people to assist you with that technical task. What should the public expect out of that piece of work? I think the, the public should, um, ex expect a, a clearer system design that, I mean, one of the things that you will have heard a lot through the select committee is that's lots of complexity around how to access services,

but fundamentally, people want to be able to stay at home as long as possible. And I think that's what we should be delivering as a system, is the opportunity to be supported to live at home as long as possible, um, and be able to transition, you know, out of hospital better. And that's where those, those palliative rehabilitative care programs would, would complement that.

Thank you Ra, Larry. Oh, thank you Minister. You've talked about the importance of keeping people at home, which is something I think we can all agree on. In Australia, it was recognized that a critical part of that workforce or the allied workforce and, uh, they were having to import workers from overseas to avoid that there was an uplift of 28.5% by the Fair Work Commission for a largely feminized workforce over the last three years.

How can you possibly expect us to be able to resolve the age care issues when you have cut pay equity claims when your government has done so? Oh, I think I'd highlight that. Um, the age care sector is largely privately delivered, publicly funded, but privately delivered. So they have employment relationships with their employers, so it's a, um, there is still a pay equity claim process that they can proceed with.

But the other component is that they, the broadly the recognition is that we need a funding model that allows the system to be sustainable. And that's what the focus of this is. If we have a sustainable funding model, which encourages the businesses to grow and expand and to meet the needs, what I would say is I've traveled the country a lot over the last year meeting of aged care providers.

Um, workforce has become less of the issue that they are managing or challenged by. Um, what they are challenged by is the funding model, um, and the, the ability to deliver the service that is needed within that funding model. And that's what we're focused on. We are aware that the pay equity, um, claim that you mentioned could not happen for a number of years.

So in reality, those pay rates are not going to change. We have a burned out workforce. We don't have transparency because of the private, uh, public funding model that you've mentioned. And if I look at the south island, there are new contracts now that means that if there are far fewer providers, uh, uh, and it does mean also the relationships with those people at home who are being fed or being showered, very intimate kind of activities, being given medication each week are encountering different workers.

Are you satisfied that, uh, your approach to pay equity and to the continuity of care is the right approach, given that in reality those pay equity claims will not be able to be taken for some years? I'm satisfied that our program work, which is to improve the funding model and the contracting environment to ensure that the system is adequately funded, is the right work program Work. And, and final question on this one. Thank you, minister.

Um, many of those aged care workers are using their own vehicles. Um, they're having to travel sometimes on directions given by people in different cities who do not understand distances their time for with their clients, and then they are effectively paying their own vehicle costs, uh, which they're not getting reimbursed for.

And the superior report pointed that out. What have you done to ameliorate that? Well, there has been, as we've said, there has been funding uplifts into the aged care sector, and that's the, the, the delivery of those services. But I'm not defending the system that you're saying that this is, there is real inefficiencies about 20% of the, um, in-home community care budget is spent on travel.

Um, so that, you know, that that is the, the challenge we've got there is a lot more efficiencies. There's a lot more opportunity to use innovation and, um, you know, to create those efficiencies around how we use the workforce. Um, and this is why the, the review is underway is to ensure that we have a fit for purpose, um, contract model and delivery of service moving forward.

Thank you. Just very, very final one. If I may, our, our recommendations made it very clear that if there is to be more investment into the sector, there needs to be transparency so the money does get to the workers, to the front line, and to overcome some of those working conditions such as the petrol and and so on and costs for workers. Are you committing that we will have in any reforms, transparency, that investment will go to the frontline rather than, uh, go to profit?

The advisory group that we've established is, uh, is those that are delivering the services, and that is why we will be ensuring that we get the right advice around what is the system that we need to deliver all the expectations, putting the consumer at the heart of those decisions as to what is the best experience they can have.

And that is around the, that has been, you know, sort of outlined in the terms of reference for this working group. Thank you Jenny, mark cro. Thank you. Uh, thank you minister. Um, palliative care is obviously a concern for many people who wish to, whether it's die at home or or access hospice service.

Um, in the far north currently, uh, there is, uh, no funding or no specialist palliative care and hospital in the far, um, what is your vision for the palliative care palliative care sector? Um, can you outline? Well, as I only have the delegation for aged care under my portfolio, so I can only speak to the palliative care service.

Um, I think that is, you know, I think it's referenced in your, your report as well. I think that palliative care is part of the process, um, around, you know, delivering quality of care, um, that is, will be factored into the program. And, um, the aged care sector has talked a lot about the challenges around delivering those services and that time limited funding, um, is not, you know, appropriate around that support.

So I think that is, um, a big part of, um, the delivery of an overall, um, you know, system design. Um, so from within the aged care sector, but I'm not sure if there is, um, further comment from the Health New Zealand around age of care. We pick that up when Health New Zealand and later that more appropriate then, um, thank you also, uh, your, your minister, your advisory group, um, which was, uh, mentioned earlier.

Um, can you give us a timeline and any indication of who's going to be? So the, uh, we'll go through a PH so, um, cabinet, um, approval will hopefully be by next week, and then we can make some announcements on that process. Um, we had, um, we are very keen to ensure that, you know, continuing on the, the work that the committee has done is that we continue this bipartisan of appreciation of, of the direction of travels.

So, um, we have, um, you know, we'll work through ways that we can ensure to continue to engage properly through its process. Um, but we will definitely have announcements within the next two weeks. And then, um, with the view to the group standing up by January next year, Supplementary supplementary, um, the announcements you're making over the next two weeks, I understand there's a meeting with Minister tomorrow.

Are, are there going to be announcements regarding retirement villages? There are repayments in the next two weeks. Um, I'm not sure of of that timeline. So Yeah. Carlos, thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, thank you Minister. My question is, you know, one of the major issues in our community is that some seniors have become increasing isolated, especially after the COVID pandemic.

How do you identify these individuals and what step did you take to help reintegrate them back to the community? I, I think that is, um, there's two components to that, which, you know, I can put my seniors hat on at the same time. But, um, I think what we've seen and what you will have seen through the select committee processes that access of care or access to the system and the complexity of the system, um, has been challenging for people.

Um, one of the areas that I see repeatedly is the enduring power of attorney. Mm. Um, the process around under which, um, care can be decisions can be made on behalf of persons who are no longer capable. That is one component. There is another piece of work that through the office for seniors that we drive around, um, elder abuse initiatives and those, um, you know, reaching those isolated communities and there's a program work that sits under that process.

But I think in terms of the, the services that we're talking about within health, this is where the system design becomes so important. The ability to access care the way care is, assessment that way, care needs are assessed, um, how that is worked forward and the, um, opportunity for all of the workforce to work at top of scope around, you know, where, whether it's pharmacists, whether it's, um, uh, uh, gps, um, nurse practitioners, how all of those focuses can make it easier for people to reach out and get help.

But there is a lot of, um, there's a, there's a mix between health issues, um, mental health issues as well as social issues that mix in there. So that's why I'm trying to drive a program work that's both looking at aged care, but also through the better later Life Action plan as Minister of Seniors is how do we integrate those solutions and work better together? Thank you, Mr. But how can we use those local group like committee care to actually reach out to those target population and, and work together?

There is a number of, you know, there is a number of contracts that exist for age concern, for example. Um, there is, um, community groups and, and, and actually, you know, volunteer groups that do that work. There is a referral process and the, um, the, there is an elder abuse, um, hotline because one of the things that we're saying is, as you all have noticed, there's a lot of self neglect, um, self deprivation where people are isolating themselves from the system.

There is mechanisms under which you can report, and that's, um, managed through the Office of Seniors. Do, sorry, checking. I got one more. Do, do you think that, um, those Friendship group or, or maybe possible kind of a daycare center for senior will be, uh, uh, one of the solution or one of the direction we look? Absolutely. Respite care is part of the consideration in this, and that would include day respite that would include, um, and there's other programs work which don't sit within my delegations around the care as strategy.

Um, but I believe that the delivery of respite care and that stay respite or support processes, um, is not only complimentary to the, the issues that you're saying, but it also provides a great deal of support for carers as well to, uh, alleviate that. So this is where, um, where I've said in the, in the terms of references about developing a system that's you can transition through and gets the right care in the right place at the right time is, is significantly important.

And the things that we need to allow people to stay at home and age with Dignity, um, should all be considered in that mix. You, you, Dr. Ferl. Thank you. Um, during the year under review, the second amendment to Smoke-Free Environments and Regulated Products Act was passed with broad support across Parliament.

Uh, it included provisions for the restrictions on banning of disposable vapes, penalties for greater penalties for under eight sales to minors restrictions on visibility and advertising. How is the implementation of those changes going? Um, it has been effective. We've seen a massive increase in the, um, prosecution, um, through, um, the infringement notice process to fine the compliance, um, has been, there's been, um, different areas where it's been, we've seen good compliance.

There have been areas where we know there are individual providers who have, um, been subject to more than one infringement. Fine. Um, that is, um, you know, going to be, continue to be the priority for compliance as we move forward. Um, I think the, um, it's still very early because that those last changes only came in in July this year.

So we're still very early in the process around assessing that the impact. Um, there have been encouraging signs, I think. Um, are you able to update the committee either now or in writing on the number of, um, uh, prosecutions? I, I would have to do that in writing. Sorry, I Don't have Thanks very much. Um, what further amendments do you plan to the, um, smoke-free environments and regulated products? So As you'll be aware, there was a advisory group that was established, their report has been published with the ministry's commentary over the top of that, that's,

um, moving forward is that, you know, what is the future state we need? I think we can all agree that it's, we've got a real anomaly around the fact that we have greater levels of regulation on vapes than we do over tobacco. So I think there's, um, a lot of work around how we do that. Um, but yeah, so I'm, I'm working through the advice from the group, um, to understand what is the, the next steps, um, you know, what are, what are the things that we need to do to a, you know, ensure there's the appropriate level of regulatory control,

um, and how do we move forward to ensure that we don't end up with the system where people go back to smoking, et cetera. So I have a few subs, if that's all right. Mr. Chair, just to be clear, the way you would respond to that is to, uh, increase the regulation of tobacco, not to decrease the regulation of vapes, which we've all, as a pa, pa parliament just agreed to increase.

No AB absolutely no, no. That, that's, we are absolutely committed to ensuring that vapes are not available for young people. Yeah, no, but I, I think that we, we do need to do something in the, in the tobacco space, and that's what the advice that we've received and we'll work through that advice. We would be love to see that. If I may, Mr. Chair, the advisory group, um, had a recommendation, a moratorium on introducing new nicotine products until there is clear independent evidence that propose new products such as oral nicotine pouches are safer than smoking

and effective in helping people who smoke to stop smoking. Do you accept that recommendation? Yeah, We, we are working through and waiting for advice from, um, the, the ministry around. You know, that, that safety criteria, and I'm expecting a report, um, early next year, you know, at, at, at sometime early next year, um, around that.
So that, that is the, the, the, the part that we want to be sure of that we are some Of the subs. Well, I'd like to continue when, Oh, I've got one from ka and

I'm actually happy for, um, to keep going. I'll go one here and then I'll go to Cam and They can put me back on list. You just, You lose your, just forfeit, you forfeit It can minister Ken, would I be able to have more than one sub? I can put you back on the list. Thank you. Uh, can you commit, minister that if independent, um, review of the evidence shows that there is no evidence that are all nicotine pouches, uh, uh, safer than smoking and effective in helping people quit, that you will not legalize them in New Zealand?

I'm committing to smoke-free 2025, but Smoke free has nothing to do with oral nicotine. So yeah, I'm committing to achieving our smoke-free 2025 target and that is the target that we, are we Going go through this whole thing all over again? Sorry guys. I have Cameron Luxon. Question minister. Have you, uh, been observing what's happening in Australians, uh, the Australia Tobacco and vape market and the perverse incentives that have come from poorly designed and burdensome regulations?

Have you been paying attention to what's happening? Yeah, very much so. And it, it is a challenging environment from the taxpayer. The recent, um, results of smoking from Australia that I've been reported is that they've seen an increase in smoking rates and the influence of organized crime into that space has been, um, significantly damaging to communities.

And so well s well, well-intentioned, uh, burdensome regulations have actually had the perverse outcome is what you've just told us. Yeah. That, and that was the indications from the increasing, um, levels of, um, smoking that we've seen in Australia And decreased emphasis, yeah, income. Yeah. Thank you. So does the minister believe that our vaping regulations are sufficient?

I think that we, we have seen really positive indications. Um, the latest ash survey has shown that our youth vaping has come down. Again, this is the third year in a row, which is that year 10 11 survey that's been continuing. Um, that, that is a really positive implication, um, and that our smoking rate has, um, continued to go down.

I think there's a lot more that we can do. Um, we still have to recognize that we, um, have the third lowest smoking rate in the OECD. So I think that's worth balancing in this conversation. Um, and the vaping, um, um, challenges that we had, I think we have made significant inroads, but again, some of those legislative changes only came in in July this year. So One more, one more. Thank you. And so daily vaping has increased in all ethnic groups from 20, 20 15, 20 16, um, year through to 23, 24 with daily things surpassing,

um, daily smoking that a few years ago. Are we doing enough, um, for those larger groups like Maori and Pacific adults where the significant increase the happening and that there is a massive disparities of like 20, 28% in Maori And 21% in Pacific adults?

I think we need to do a lot more to bring smoking rates down. Um, Maori and Pacifica smoking rates, um, are still not where we need them to be. They're significantly higher than the other population groups. Um, we still see very high smoking rates in older people. Um, so that is the, the target demographic. We definitely need to keep vapes away from people, but we cannot ignore that vaping is less harmful than smoking cigarettes.

And I've never said that it's not harmful and it's less harmful. And therefore, in terms of protecting health outcomes overall, I think that's the focus we need to continue to have. Go sir, Dr. Chong, and then I'll come back to you Dr. Ver Thank Mr. Chair. So minister, have you actually got time to look into my member's bill on vaping about changing the licensing system, instructing the favor and also adding a, a health discri on the product.

Do you think those are the direction the government actually looking at? I, I think again, that, you know, that this working group and the advice that, that I've received and what is the direction of travel will take into account all of those issues. We still have to balance the fact that access to vapes has contributed to our reduction in smoking rates.

So we need to ensure that access to vapes is directed at those that we want to ensure don't smoke, um, we do not want you to have access to vapes, and we will continue to make that the priority. Um, Because one of my proposal is about changing the licensing system from a lifetime licensing system to become a renewable, uh, renewable system licensing system.

Do you think this is more practical or there's something you are interested to looking into it and, And as I said through the recommendations we've got from the advisory route, what that regulatory regime is? Um, I think that what you have focused on is, again, this, and it's, is what I touched on with Dr. Verell before, is that we have a system where we have massively focused on vape regulation and we need to balance that with, um, how, how do we manage tobacco products, which are the most harmful in that regulatory framework.

So, um, what I'm keen to do is develop a system that allows us, um, to continue to meet our target for smoke-free. Um, and how we deal that is, um, you know, that's what the advice has been sought. Mm-hmm. Yeah, Minister the parliament has really struggled over seven years to regulate vapes and to address this nicotine addiction, which is a health harm in children.

Why would you consider introducing a new form of nicotine to New Zealand that will only create another pool of addiction in young people? I, I think that you, you're conflating a couple of issues there. One is that seven years, seven years that we had to get, um, the vaping regulation.

We didn't have any regulation to start with and we didn't do it very well at all as we went through the process. And this is the, the challenge we had. We went from no regulation to reactive regulation around how we were going to deal with this. That is, you know, we, I think we have seen, we have seen a significant shift in our outcomes, um, and vaping rates.

So I think we have, um, headed in the right direction now. We have seen that those youth vaping rates are coming down, but if we look at from an evidence-based point of view, there are two countries that have lower smoking rates than us, and they have other vaping product, other tobacco, uh, nicotine products.

Um, we have used nicotine replacement treatments throughout the process of reducing our smoking rates. So therefore the process that we are looking at is, as you've said, an evidence-based, um, system around how do we ensure that we have a huge number between that 55 and 64 age group and you know, 45 to 64 age group that are still long-term addicted smokers.

And so therefore having products available to them as a pathway away from smoking should be something we consider. Well, if you are happy to have an evidence-based approach as you imply notwithstanding the considerable misdirection from that answer, why won't you accept the group's recommendation that you'll only introduce oral nicotine products If there's evidence that they are safer than tobacco and reduce smoking, why won't you accept, accept an evidence-based re recommendation?

I, as I've said, I'm not, I'm not saying I'm not accepting a re um, an it should be Easy, very close. Well, it, it is, but there is a number of recommendations in that report and we will work through as to how we can operationalize those recommendations and how it ultimately delivers. We accept People say no to drugs every day, just say no to this.

Yeah. I've got to know that there's a saturation of vape stores in lower, so economic communities, for example, which has 19 vape stores and they're small Northland town. Do you think that Kaiko, he needs this many vape stores and does the minister, or can the minister help that, that community and other communities like it and councils to be able to control how many vape stores are actually in the community?

Correct. Yeah. And, and that's, I think that's exactly what, you know, we're talking about, about the future regime is what is the regulatory system to control the conduct of those tools. At the moment, the only tool we have is infringement notices. So how do we deal with noncompliance stores? How do we manage that process? Um, you know, the reducing the displays, et cetera, the increasing the penalties for non-compliance.

You know, they can be a deterrent, but I don't think they're having the impact that we need around how do we ensure that there is a, a system that beyond just, you know, I'm, I'm now licensed, how do we have a system that manages non-compliance and deals with their ability to do business? And therefore the same thing applies because similarly, you have just as many, um, stores that sell cigarettes.

So how do we manage a system that is, um, going to control and, and provide the transparency we need? Ultimately this is about reduction in harm and we need to ensure that is the priority. So I think keeping the kids out and that's what we've seen in the results, but the future state and that's why I'm really keen to, um, take the time we need to develop what that future state looks like because we've got to think beyond, you know, getting to our smoke free target.

What does that future state look like so that we don't end up in a system where illicit tobacco or other products end up, um, replacing things and creating a new addiction stream. So there, there is a lot of things to consider and the smoke Get the dirty ashtray as as well. Thank minister, thank you for making yourself available this morning for answering questions.
Oh, we're actually just overtime and we have minister waiting to come in, so thank you very much. Thanks for

my goodness. Uh, are we able to get some more water please? Some. Well, we've got one there. You alright minister? Very good, thank you. Excellent. Let's, okay on tour. Welcome honorable minister for General Clark Bramley, everyone else and gallery.

Fantastic. Have you all here, uh, we have you in here for 45 minutes this morning. I'll allow you to make some opening remarks and then we'll open it up to committee members for questions. Got it. Good. Marina, uh, good morning everyone. Uh, thank you, uh, Mr. Chair and thanks all the members of the Health Select committee and all the work that, um, you do.

Look, I'm, um, straight into it, maybe assisted, uh, by leadership and officials here today. I'm sure I don't need to introduce them as they're probably, um, uh, familiar faces to you now as, uh, health ministers that have already been, um, before me and associate health ministers as well.

I do want to, um, take this opportunity though to think officials and actually all those who are at the front line, um, working hard for better mental health, um, in New Zealand. Looking back, uh, at what we've achieved over the last year. In preparation for this, um, very short introduction, uh, I was, uh, impressed by the amount of work the government's, uh, mental health program.

Three key deliverable actions, faster access to support more frontline workers and a better crisis response. Uh, we've increased the ringfence for mental health and addiction, $2.4 billion when we came into office. Now 2.8, uh, announced $28 million for rolling out co response mental health teams.

Further $61 million recently for bolstering crisis response. That will be more frontline workers in our crisis assessment and treatment teams, as well as more peer led acute alternatives for inpatient services as well. Uh, a $48 million refresh of the first ever mental health and addiction workforce plan.

This country seen delivering outstanding results with some mental health, uh, professions. The vacancy rates in health New Zealand dropping by 65% since coming into office. The meth action plan, $30 million grounded in feedback from the frontline, how we'll reduce demand for meth and support those who reach out in the time of need through, uh, addiction.

We've got the FASD action plan six and a half million dollars funded out of the increase in the alcohol levy first government in 15 years to increase the alcohol levy. And we've funded, uh, uh, fee to our alcohol spectrum disorder program. Um, out of that as well, uh, would refresh the next five years of the suicide prevention action plan, an extra $16 million, uh, on top of the existing 2021 new health led actions in that plan.

And 13 new cross-agency actions, uh, as well. Uh, something I'm quite proud of is the refresh of the eating disorder, uh, strategy. That's something I heard loud and clear when we're in opposition.
